questions.question: The line through the points (a, b) and (- a, - b) passes through the point
questions.question_raw: The line through the points (a, b) and (- a, - b) passes through the point
questions.explanation:
Equation of a line passing through (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is given by
{tex}y-y_1 \over y_2-y_1{/tex}={tex}x - x_1\over x_2-x_1{/tex} 
Substituting the given values we get,
{tex}y-b\over -b-b{/tex} = {tex}x-a \over -a-a{/tex}  
That is {tex}y-b \over -2b {/tex} = {tex}x-a \over -2a{/tex} 
On cross multiplying and rearranging we get,
bx - ay = 0
If this passes through the given point (a2, ab) then
b(a2) - a(ab) = 0

questions.question: The image of the point ($\alpha {\rm{ }},{\rm{ }}\beta $) with respect to the line x + y = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The image of the point ({tex}\alpha {\rm{ }},{\rm{ }}\beta {/tex}) with respect to the line x + y = 0 is
questions.explanation:
The equation of the line which is perpendicular to the given line x + y = 0 is x - y = 0
The point of intersection of these two lines is (0, 0) which is the origin
This point of intersection is the midpoint of the given point ({tex}\alpha,\beta{/tex}) and its image, say (a, b)
Therefore {tex}\alpha +a\over 2{/tex} 
This implies a = {tex}-\alpha{/tex} 
Similarly {tex}\beta+b\over 2{/tex} = 0
This implies b = {tex}-\beta{/tex} 
Therefore {tex}(-\alpha, -\beta){/tex} is the image.

questions.question: The straight lines x + y = 0, 3x - y – 4 = 0, x + 3y – 4 = 0 form a triangle which is
questions.question_raw: The straight lines x + y = 0, 3x - y – 4 = 0, x + 3y – 4 = 0 form a triangle which is
questions.explanation:
The lines formed by these lines is a right-angled, triangle.
Two lines are perpendicular to each other if the product of their slopes is -1.
Slope of the lines  3x - y – 4 = 0, x + 3y – 4 = 0 are 3 and {tex}\frac{-1}{3}{/tex} respectively.
The product of these slopes is -1
Hence the lines 3x - y – 4 = 0, x + 3y – 4 = 0  are perpendicular to each other.
Therefore the triangle formed by these lines is a right-angled triangle.

Substituting the values,
{tex}2\over 6{/tex} ={tex}-3\over -9{/tex}{tex}\ne{/tex}{tex}5\over 7{/tex} 
Hence they are parallel lines.

questions.question: The line passing through (1, 1) and parallel to the line 2x – 3y + 5 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The line passing through (1, 1) and parallel to the line 2x – 3y + 5 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
The required line is parallel to the given line, hence it has same slope.
Therefore the equation of the required line is 2x - 3y + k = 0
Since it passes through (1, 1)
2(1) - 3(1) + k = 0
Therefore k = 1
Hence the equation of the required line is 2x - 3y + 1 = 0

questions.question: The straight lines x + y - 4 = 0, 3x + y – 4 = 0, x - 3y – 4 = 0 form a triangle which is
questions.question_raw: The straight lines x + y - 4 = 0, 3x + y – 4 = 0, x - 3y – 4 = 0 form a triangle which is
questions.explanation:
The triangle formed by these lines is a right-angled triangle.
If the lines are perpendicular to each other, then the product of their slopes is -1
The slope of lines  3x + y – 4 = 0, x - 3y – 4 = 0 are  -3 and {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} respectively.
The product of the slopes is -1.
Hence these two lines are perpendicular to each other.
This infers that the triangle formed by these lines is a right angled triangle.

The angle made by the line y = 0 with the Y-axis is 90o. Therefore the acute angle between the lines is 45o.

questions.question: The vertices of a triangle are (0, 3), (-3, 0) and (3, 0). The orthocenter of the triangle is
questions.question_raw: The vertices of a triangle are (0, 3), (-3, 0) and (3, 0). The orthocenter of the triangle is
questions.explanation:
From the given points it is clear that these points form a right-angled isosceles triangle.
The orthocentre of all right angle lies on its right-angle vertex.
Here the right angle vertex is (0, 3)
Hence the orthocentre is (0, 3)

questions.question: The lines x + ( k - 1)y + 1 = 0 and 2x + k^2y - 1 = 0 are at right angles if
questions.question_raw: The lines x + ( k - 1)y + 1 = 0 and 2x + k2y - 1 = 0 are at right angles if
questions.explanation:
If the lines are at right angles to each other, then the product of their slopes = -1. Slope of any line = -(coefficient of x/coeffiecient of y)
Therefore the slope of line 1 = -{tex}\frac{1}{k-1}{/tex}
The slope of line 2 = {tex}\frac{-2}{k^2}{/tex}
Therefore {tex}-1 \over (k-1){/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}-2 \over k^2{/tex} = - 1
That is k2 (k - 1) = -2
i.e; k3 - k2 + 2 = 0
On factorizing we get (k + 1)(k2 - 2k - 2) = 0
This imlpies k + 1 is a factor, hence k = -1
Hence they are at right angles if k = -1

questions.question: The lines y = mx, y + 2x = 0, y = 2x + $\lambda $ and y = - mx + $\lambda $ form a rhombus if m =
questions.question_raw: The lines y = mx, y + 2x = 0, y = 2x + {tex}\lambda {/tex} and y = - mx + {tex}\lambda {/tex} form a rhombus if m =
questions.explanation:
Rhombus is a parallelogram in which the opposite sides are equal and parallel.
Therefore the lines y = mx and y = -2x are parallel, similarly y = 2x + {tex}\lambda{/tex} and y = -mx + {tex}\lambda{/tex} are parallel.
If two lines are equal, then their slopes are equal.
This implies m = -2

questions.question: Two points (a, 0) and (0, b) are joined by a straight line. Another point on this line is
questions.question_raw: Two points (a, 0) and (0, b) are joined by a straight line. Another point on this line is
questions.explanation:
The slope of the line joining the points (a, 0) and (0, b) is [{tex}\frac{[b-0]}{[0-a]}{/tex} = -({tex}\frac{b}{a}{/tex})
Hence the equation of the line is y = (-{tex}\frac{b}{a}{/tex})x + b
i.e; ay = -bx + ab
Substituting the x coordinate 3a in the place of x in the above equation we get y = -2b
Hence (3a, -2b) is another point on the line.

questions.question: The coordinates of the foot of perpendicular from (0, 0) upon the line x + y = 2 are
questions.question_raw: The coordinates of the foot of perpendicular from (0, 0) upon the line x + y = 2 are
questions.explanation:
The equation of the line perpendicular to the given line is x - y + k = 0
Since it passes through the origin, 
0 - 0 + k = 0
Therefore, k = 0
Hence the equation of the line is x - y = 0
On solving these two equations we get x = 1 and y = 1
The point of intersection of these two lines is (1, 1)
Hence the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 1)

questions.question: A line is drawn through the points (3, 4) and (5, 6). If the is extended to a point whose ordinate is – 1, then the abscissa of that point is
questions.question_raw: A line is drawn through the points (3, 4) and (5, 6). If the is extended to a point whose ordinate is – 1, then the abscissa of that point is
questions.explanation:
The slope of the given line is {tex}y_2 - y_1 \over x_2-x_1{/tex} = {tex}6-4 \over 5 -3{/tex} = 1
Therefore {tex}4-(-1) \over 3-x{/tex} = 1
That is 4 + 1 = 3 - x
Therefore x = -2

questions.question: The line x + y – 6 = 0 is the right bisector of the segment [PQ]. If P is the point (4, 3), then the point Q is
questions.question_raw: The line x + y – 6 = 0 is the right bisector of the segment [PQ]. If P is the point (4, 3), then the point Q is
questions.explanation:
Equation of the line which is perpendicular to the given line x + y = 6 is x - y + k = 0
Since it passes through the point (4, 3)
4 - 3 + k = 0
Therefore k = -1
Hence the equation of the line segment PQ is x - y - 1 = 0
On solving these two lines we get x = {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex} and y = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
This point of intersection is the midpoint of the line segment PQ.
That is {tex}4+x \over 2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex} Hence x = 3
Similarly {tex}3+y \over 2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}. Hence y = 2
Hence the coordinates of the point Q is (3, 2).

questions.question: The acute angle between the lines ax + by + c = 0 and (a + b)x = (a – b)y , a $ \ne $b , is
questions.question_raw: The acute angle between the lines ax + by + c = 0 and (a + b)x = (a – b)y , a {tex} \ne {/tex}b , is
questions.explanation:
The slope of the first line is -({tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex}) and slope of the second line is {tex}a+b \over a-b{/tex} 
Angle between the lines is {tex}tan\theta{/tex}  = {tex}|m_2 - m_1|\over 1+ m_1m_2{/tex} 
Let m1 = -({tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex}) and m2 = {tex}a+b \over a-b{/tex} 
Hence {tex}tan \theta{/tex} =[ {tex}(-a/b) - (a+b)/a-b)\over1 +(-a/b)(a+b)/(a-b){/tex}]
On simplifying we get, 
tan {tex} \theta{/tex} = 1
This implies {tex}\theta{/tex} = 45o

questions.question: The triangle formed by the lines x + y = 1, 2x + 3y – 6 = 0 and 4x – y + 4 = 0 lies in
questions.question_raw: The triangle formed by the lines x + y = 1, 2x + 3y – 6 = 0 and 4x – y + 4 = 0 lies in
questions.explanation:
On solving line 1 and line 2 we get x = -3 and y = 4. Hence the point of intersection is  (-3, 4)
On solving line 2 and line 3 we get x = ({tex}\frac{-3}{7}{/tex}) and y = {tex}\frac{16}{7}{/tex}. Hence the point of intersection is ({tex}\frac{-3}{7}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{16}{7}{/tex})
On solving line 3 and line 1 we get x = {tex}\frac{-3}{7}{/tex} and y = {tex}\frac{-8}{7}{/tex}. Hence the point of intersection is ({tex}\frac{-3}{7}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{-8}{7}{/tex})
All the above points lie in the second quadrant. Hence the triangle formed by these lines also lie in the second quadrant.

questions.question: The area of triangle formed by the lines y = x, y = 2x and y = 3x + 4 is
questions.question_raw: The area of triangle formed by the lines y = x, y = 2x and y = 3x + 4 is
questions.explanation:
Area of the triangle formed by the coordinates (x1, y1), (x2, y2) and (x3, y3) is given by
{tex}1\over 2{/tex}[x1(y2 - y3) + x2(y3 - y1) + x3(y1 - y2)]
On solving the lines 1 and 2, the point of intersection is (0, 0)
On solving the lines 2 and 3, the point of intersection is (4, 8)
On solving the line 3 and 1 , the point of intersection is (-2, -2)
Now substituting the values to find the area of the triangle,
Area = {tex}1\over 2{/tex}| [0 + 4(-2 - 0) + (-2)(0 - 8)] |
= 4 sq units

Similarly solving lines 3 and 4, the point of intersection is (-1, 2)
On solving lines 1 and 4 the point of intersection is (-1, 2)

questions.question: The orthogonal projection of the point (2, - 3) on the line x + y = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The orthogonal projection of the point (2, - 3) on the line x + y = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Equation of the line which is perpendicular to the given line is x - y + k = 0
Since this line passes through (2, -3)
2-(-3) + k = 0
This implies k = -5
Hence the equation og the line is x - y = 5
On solving the lines x + y = 0 and x - y = 5, we get the point of intersection as x = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and y = {tex}\frac{-5}{2}{/tex}
Hence ({tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{-5}{2}{/tex}) is the coordinates of orthogonal projection.

questions.question: The lines 8x + 4y = 1, 8x + 4y = 5, 4x + 8y = 3, 4x + 8y = 7 form a
questions.question_raw: The lines 8x + 4y = 1, 8x + 4y = 5, 4x + 8y = 3, 4x + 8y = 7 form a
questions.explanation:
On solving the equations  8x + 4y = 1 and 4x + 8y = 3, we get the point of intersection as ({tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{5}{12}{/tex})
On solving the equations 8x + 4y = 5 and 4x + 8y = 7, we get the point of intersection as ({tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex})
On solving the equations 8x + 4y = 1 and 4x + 8y = 7, we get the point of intersection as ({tex}\frac{-5}{12}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{13}{12}{/tex})
On solving the equations 8x + 4y = 5 and 4x + 8y = 3, we the point of intersection as ({tex}\frac{7}{12}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{1}{12}{/tex})
Let the points A(({tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{5}{12}{/tex}), B({tex}\frac{7}{12}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{1}{12}{/tex}) C({tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}) and D({tex}\frac{-5}{12}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{13}{12}{/tex}) be the vertices of the quadrilateral
Since the slopes of the opposite sides are equal the quadrilateral is a parallelogram
The slope of the diagonal AC is {tex}5/12 - 3/4\over -1/2 - 1/4{/tex} = 1
The slope of the diagonal BD is {tex}1/2 - 13/12\over 7/12 - (-5/12){/tex} = -1
Since the product of the slopes is -1, the diagonals are perpendicular to each other.
Hence the parallelogram is a rhombus.

Then, by distance formula we have
(x - 2)2 + 32 = c2
x2 - 4x + 4 + 9 - c2 = 0
x2 - 4x + 13 - c2 = 0
x will be real if the b2 - 4ac  {tex}\ge{/tex}0
i.e; 16 - 4(13 - c2) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
i.e; c2 - 9 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
i.e; |c| {tex}\ge{/tex} 3
Hence there is no point.

questions.question: The equations of the lines through (1, 1) and making angles of 45^o with the line x + y = 0
questions.question_raw: The equations of the lines through (1, 1) and making angles of 45o with the line x + y = 0
questions.explanation:
If the lines make equal angles of 45o with the given line, x + y = 0.
Then these lines must be perpendicular with each other.
This is possible only when the two lines are parallel to X-axis and Y-axis.
That is the equations should be x = a constant and y  = a constant.
Since it passes through (1, 1)
The equations should be x = 1 or x - 1 = 0 and y = 1 or y - 1 = 0

questions.question: The number of lines that are parallel to 2x + 6y – 7 = 0 and have an intercept 10 units between the coordinate axis is :
questions.question_raw: The number of lines that are parallel to 2x + 6y – 7 = 0 and have an intercept 10 units between the coordinate axis is :
questions.explanation:
The slope of the given line 2x + 6y = 7 is {tex}\frac{-1}{3}{/tex}
Hence the line which is parallel to the above line is 
y = ({tex}\frac{-1}{3}{/tex})x + c
That is the y-intercept is (0, c) and the x-intercept is (3c, 0)
Using the distance formula
d2 = (0 - 3c)2 + (3c - 0)2
= 10c2
Since the distance is given as 10, then
100 = 10c2
Therefore c = {tex} \pm{/tex}10
Since two values are possible, two lines can be drawn.

questions.question: The area of the quadrilateral formed by the lines |x| + |y| = 1 is
questions.question_raw: The area of the quadrilateral formed by the lines |x| + |y| = 1 is
questions.explanation:
Equations of the lines are
x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, then x + y = 1
x {tex} \le{/tex} 0, y {tex}\le{/tex} 0, then x + y = -1
x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, y {tex}\le{/tex} 0, then x - y = 2
x {tex}\le{/tex} 0, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, then x - y = -2
Clearly these lines form a square, whose coordinates are (1, 1), (1, -1),(-1, -1), (-1, 1)
Hence its area is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} [{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}]1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 2 sq units

questions.question: The line segment joining the points (1, 2) and (-2, 1) is divided by the line 3x + 4y = 7 in the ratio
questions.question_raw: The line segment joining the points (1, 2) and (-2, 1) is divided by the line 3x + 4y = 7 in the ratio
questions.explanation:
Let the line divide in the ratio k:1
By applying section formula {tex}k.x_2 -1.x_1\over k+1{/tex}, {tex}k.y_2-1.y_1\over k+1{/tex}
Substituting the values
{tex}-2k+1\over k+1{/tex}, {tex}k+2\over k+1{/tex}
This given line 3x + 4y = 7 passes through this point.
Hence, 3({tex}-2k+1\over k+1{/tex}) + 4({tex}k+2\over k+1{/tex} )= 7
On simplifying, we get,

questions.question: A line L passes through the points (1, 1) and (2, 0) and another line M which is perpendicular to L passes through the point (1/2, 0). The area of the triangle formed by these lines with y axis is :
questions.question_raw: A line L passes through the points (1, 1) and (2, 0) and another line M which is perpendicular to L passes through the point (1/2, 0). The area of the triangle formed by these lines with y axis is :
questions.explanation:
The equation of the line joining the two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is
{tex}y - y_1 \over y_2 - y_1{/tex} = {tex}x-x_1\over x_2 - x_1{/tex}
The given points are (1, 1) and (2, 0)
On substituting the values we get
{tex}y-1\over 0-1{/tex} = {tex}x-1\over 2-1 {/tex}
On simplifying we get,
x + y - 2 = 0

Slope of the median AD is {tex}b-0\over (a/2)-0{/tex}  =  -{tex}2b\over a{/tex}
Sope of BE =  {tex}(a/2)-0\over(b/2)-0{/tex}= {tex}b\over a{/tex}
If BE and AD are perpendicular to each other then their product is -1
i.e;- {tex}2b\over a{/tex} x {tex}b\over a{/tex}  = -1

a = {tex}\pm\sqrt2{/tex}b

y = mx + a/m

y2 = x implies a = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

That is the y-intercept is {tex}\sqrt \frac 14{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
Hence the equation of the line is y = {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex}

questions.question_raw: The points (- a, -b), (0, 0), (a, b) and (a2, ab) are
questions.explanation:
Consider the points (a, b), (0, 0), (-a, -b)
It is clear that (0, 0) is the midpoint of (a, b) and (-a, -b)
This implies that these three points are collinear
Now consider the points,
(a, b), (a2, ab) and (-a, -b)
If three points are collinear then the area of the triangle formed by these points is zero.
That is {tex}1\over 2{/tex}[x1(y2 - y3) + x2(y3 - y1) + x3(y1 - y2)]= 0
Substituting the values
{tex}1\over 2{/tex}[a(a + b) + a2(-b - b) + (-a)(b - ab)]
On expanding and simplifying we get the value to be zero.
Hence these points are collinear.

Substituting the values we get
5 = {tex}1\over 2{/tex}[(x(1 + 2) + 2(-2 - y) + 3(y - 1)]
That is 10 = 3x + y - 7
or 3x + y = 17

{tex}\therefore{/tex} The distance of a point P(2, 3) from the line x + y - 7 = 0, is d = {tex}\frac{| 2+3-7|}{\sqrt{1+1}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
Now, is {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR,
QR ={tex}\sqrt{16-d^{2}}=\sqrt{16-2}=\sqrt{14}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \tan \theta=\frac{d}{Q R}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{14}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}=\left|\frac{m+1}{1-m}\right|{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \tan \theta=\left|\frac{m_{2}-m_{1}}{1+m_{1} m_{2}}\right|\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{m+1}{1-m}=\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{m+1}{1-m}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}} \text { or } \frac{m+1}{1-m}=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}{/tex}

questions.question: Suppose that the points (h, k), (1, 2) and (-3, 4) lie on the line L_1. If a line L_2 passing through the points (h, k) and (4, 3) is perpendicular to L_1, then $\frac{k}{h}$ equals

{tex}m_{1}=\frac{4-2}{-3-1}=\frac{k-2}{h-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad m_{1}=\frac{-1}{2}=\frac{k-2}{h-1}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(k - 2) = -1(h - 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2k - 4 = -h + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h + 2k = 5 ...(ii)
and slope of line L2 joining points (h,k) and
(4, 3), is {tex}m_{2}=\frac{3-k}{4-h}{/tex} ...(iii)
Since, lines L1 and L2 are perpendicular to each other.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} m1m2 = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{3-k}{4-h}\right){/tex} = -1 [from Eqs. (i) and (iii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 - k = 8 - 2h
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2h - k = 5 ...(iv)
On solving Eqs. (ii) and (iv), we get (h, k) = (3, 1)
So, {tex}\frac{k}{h}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: Let k be an integer such that the triangle with vertices (k, - 3k), (5, k) and (- k, 2) has area 28 sq units. Then, the orthocentre of this triangle is at the point
questions.question_raw: Let k be an integer such that the triangle with vertices (k, - 3k), (5, k) and (- k, 2) has area 28 sq units. Then, the orthocentre of this triangle is at the point
questions.explanation:
Given, vertices of triangle are (k, - 3k), (5, k) and (- k, 2)
{tex}\begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {k} & {-3 k} & {1} \\ {5} & {k} & {1} \\ {-k} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right|=\pm 28\\ &\Rightarrow \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {k} & {-3 k} & {1} \\ {5} & {k} & {1} \\ {-k} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right|=\pm 56 \end{aligned}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k(k - 2) + 3k (5 + k) + 1(10 + K2) = {tex}\pm 56{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k2 - 2k + 15k + 3k2 + 10 + k2 = {tex}\pm 56{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5k2 + 13k + 10 = {tex}\pm 56{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5k2 + 13 - 66 = 0
or 5k2 + 13k - 46 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 2 [{tex}\because{/tex} k {tex}\in{/tex} I]
Thus, the coordinates of vertices of triangle are
A(2, - 6), B(5, 2) and C (- 2, 2).
Now, equation of altitude from vertex A is
{tex}y-(-6)=\frac{-1}{\left(\frac{2-2}{-2-5}\right)}(x-2) \Rightarrow x=2{/tex} ...(i)

Equation of altitude from vertex C is
{tex}y-2=\frac{-1}{\left[\frac{2-(-6)}{5-2}\right]}[x-(-2)]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + 8y - 10 = 0 ...(ii)
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get x = 2 and {tex}y=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Orthocentre = {tex}\left(2, \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}

questions.question:
The point (4, 1) undergoes the following three transformations successively
 Reflection about the line y = x.
 Transformation through a distance 2 units along the positive direction of X-axis.
 Rotation through an angle $\frac{\pi}{4}$ about the origin in the counter clockwise direction.
Then, the final position of the point is given by the coordinates.
questions.question_raw:
The point (4, 1) undergoes the following three transformations successively

  1. Reflection about the line y = x.
  2. Transformation through a distance 2 units along the positive direction of X-axis.
  3. Rotation through an angle {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} about the origin in the counter clockwise direction.

Then, the final position of the point is given by the coordinates.
questions.explanation:
Let B, C, D be the position of the point A(4,1) after the three operations I, II and III, respectively. Then, B is (1, 4), C(1 + 2, 4) i.e. (3, 4). The point D is obtained from C by rotating the coordinate axes through an angle {tex}\frac {\pi}{4}{/tex} in anti-clockwise direction.
Therefore, the coordinates of D are given by
X = 3 cos{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} - 4 sin{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
and Y = 3 sin{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + 4 cos{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Coordinates of D are {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{7}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}

 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}|h + k - 2| = 6

questions.question: Two vertices of a triangle are (-2, -1) and (3, 2) and the third vertex lies on the line x + y = 5. If the area of the triangle is 4 square units, then the third vertex is
questions.question_raw: Two vertices of a triangle are (-2, -1) and (3, 2) and the third vertex lies on the line x + y = 5. If the area of the triangle is 4 square units, then the third vertex is

It is given that the area of the triangle with vertices (h, k), (-2, -1) and (3, 2) is 4 square units.
{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[h(-1 - 2) - 3(-1 - k) - 2(2 - k)] = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3h - 5k + 1 = {tex}\pm{/tex}8
Taking positive sign, we get,
3h - 5k + 1 = 8
3h - 5k + 7 = 0 ...(i)
Taking negative sign, we get,
3h - 5k + 9 = 0 ...(ii)
The vertex (h, k) lies on the line x + y = 5.
h + k - 5 = 0 ...(iii)
On solving (i) and (iii), we find (4, 1) to be the coordinates of the third vertex.
Similarly, on solving (ii) and (iii), we find the required vertex (0, 5) or (4, 1).

questions.question: P is a point on the line y + 2x = 1 and Q and R are two points on the line 3y + 6x = 6 such that the triangle PQR is an equilateral triangle. Area of this triangle is equal to :
questions.question_raw: P is a point on the line y + 2x = 1 and Q and R are two points on the line 3y + 6x = 6 such that the triangle PQR is an equilateral triangle. Area of this triangle is equal to :
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR is equilateral triangle
Given Lines: y + 2x = 1; 3y + 6x = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y + 2x = 2
As both lines have same slope thus given lines are parallel.
To find: Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR
For finding Area, we would do so by finding altitude of {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR which would be simply the perpendicular distance between both given line because both lines are parallel.
So, Distance formula for {tex}\perp{/tex} distance between two lines = d = {tex}\frac{\left|{c}_{1}-{c}_{2}\right|}{\sqrt{{a}^{2}+{b}^{2}}}{/tex}
So distance between given lines: {tex}\frac{|2-1|}{\sqrt{2^{2}+1^{2}}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex} = Altitude of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR
Now, Area of equilateral triangle in terms of altitude 'h' = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}^{2}}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
So Area of  {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^{2}}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{5 \sqrt{3}}{/tex}

questions.question_raw: The distance of the line 2x - 3y = 4 from the point (1, 1) in the direction of the line x + y = 1 is :
questions.explanation:
Equation of line parallel to x + y = 1 is x + y + c = 0
Let it passes through (1, 1) so the line is along direction of x + y = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 + 1 + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y - 2 = 0 ...(i)
Given line is 2x - 3y - 4 = 0 ...(ii)
Solving (i) and (ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, y = 0
So the point of intersection is (2, 0)
So the distance of (1, 1) from 2x - 3y - 4 = 0 along x + y + 1 = 0 is
{tex}=\sqrt{(2-1)^{2}+(0-1)^{2}}{/tex}
 {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question: If x_1, x_2, x_3 as well as y_1, y_2, y_3, are in G.P. with the same common ratio, then the points (x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2) and (x_3, y_3).
questions.question_raw: If x1, x2, x3 as well as y1, y2, y3, are in G.P. with the same common ratio, then the points (x1, y1), (x2, y2) and (x3, y3).
questions.explanation:
Since x1, x2, x3 are in GP. and y1, y2, y3 are also in GP. Common ratio of both GP.’s are same.
Let it be r. Then
x2 = x1r, x3 = x1r2 and so is y2 = y1r, y3 = y1r2
{tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll} x_{1} & y_{1} & 1 \\ x_{2} & y_{2} & 1 \\ x_{3} & y_{3} & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}r \cdot r^{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x_{1} & y_{1} & 1 \\ x_{1} & y_{1} & 1 \\ x_{1} & y_{1} & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The points lie on a line, i.e., they are collinear.

questions.question: The sides of a rhombus ABCD are parallel to the lines, x - y + 2 = 0 and 7x - y + 3 = 0. If the diagonals of the rhombus intersect at P(1, 2) and the vertex A (different from the origin) is on the y-axis, then the ordinate of A is:
questions.question_raw: The sides of a rhombus ABCD are parallel to the lines, x - y + 2 = 0 and 7x - y + 3 = 0. If the diagonals of the rhombus intersect at P(1, 2) and the vertex A (different from the origin) is on the y-axis, then the ordinate of A is:
questions.explanation:
Let the coordinated be (0, c)
Equations of the given lines are
x - y + 2 = 0 and 7x - y + 3 = 0
We know that the diagonals of the rhombus will be parallel to the angle bisectors of the two given lines;
y = x + 2 and y = 7x + 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} equation of angle bisectors is given as:
{tex}\frac{x-y+2}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\pm \frac{7 x-y+3}{5 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}
5x - 5y + 10 = {tex}\pm{/tex}(7x - y + 3)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Parallel equations of the diagonals are 2x + 4y - 7 = 0 and 12x - 6y + 13 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} slopes of diagonals are {tex}\frac {-1}{2}{/tex} and 2.
Now, slope of the diagonal from A(0, c) and passing through P(1, 2) is (2 - c)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2 - c = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0 (not possible)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2 - c = {tex}\frac {-1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = {tex}\frac {5}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ordinate of A is {tex}\frac {5}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: If the perpendicular bisector of the line segment joining the points P(1, 4) and Q(k, 3) has y-intercept equal to -4, then a value of k is:
questions.question_raw: If the perpendicular bisector of the line segment joining the points P(1, 4) and Q(k, 3) has y-intercept equal to -4, then a value of k is:
questions.explanation:
The midpoint of line segment PQ be {tex}\left(\frac{k+1}{2}, \frac{7}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The slope of the perpendicular line passing through (0, -4) and {tex}\left(\frac{k+1}{2}, \frac{7}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{7}{2}+4}{\frac{k+1}{2}-0}=\frac{15}{k+1}{/tex}
The slope of PQ = {tex}\frac{4-3}{1-k}=\frac{1}{1-k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{15}{1+k} \times \frac{1}{1-k}{/tex} = -1
1 - k2 = -15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}\pm{/tex}4

questions.question: A triangle ABC lying in the first quadrant has two vertices as A(1, 2) and B(3, 1). If $\angle$BAC = 90^o, and ar ($\triangle$ABC) = $5 \sqrt{5}$ sq. units, then the abscissa of the vertex C is:
questions.question_raw: A triangle ABC lying in the first quadrant has two vertices as A(1, 2) and B(3, 1). If {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 90o, and ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) = {tex}5 \sqrt{5}{/tex} sq. units, then the abscissa of the vertex C is:
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC be in the first quadrant
Slope of line AB = {tex}- \frac {1}{2}{/tex}
Slope of line AC = 2
Length of AB = {tex}\sqrt 5{/tex}

It is given that ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) = {tex}5 \sqrt{5}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} AB {tex}\times{/tex} AC = {tex}5 \sqrt{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = 10
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Coordinate of vertex C = (1 + 10 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}, 2 + 10sin {tex}\theta{/tex})
{tex}\because{/tex} tan{tex}\theta{/tex} = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex}, sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Coordinate of C = (1 + {tex}2 \sqrt{5}{/tex}, 2 + {tex}4 \sqrt{5}{/tex})
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Abscissa of vertex C is 1 + {tex}2 \sqrt{5}{/tex}

The centroid of the triangle is the intersection of the mesians. Here the medians x = 4 and x + 4 and x + y = 5 intersect at G (4, 1).
The area of triangle {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}AGC
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} [1(1 - 3) + 4(3 - 2) + 4(2 - 1)] = 9

questions.question: Let $A B C$ be a triangle with $A(-3,1)$ and $\angle {ACB}=\theta, 0 \lt \theta \lt \frac{\pi}{2}$. If the equation of the median through B is $2 x+y-3=0$ and the equation of angle bisector of C is $7 x-4 y-1=0$, then $\tan \theta$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}A B C{/tex} be a triangle with {tex}A(-3,1){/tex} and {tex}\angle {ACB}=\theta, 0 \lt \theta \lt \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}. If the equation of the median through B is {tex}2 x+y-3=0{/tex} and the equation of angle bisector of C is {tex}7 x-4 y-1=0{/tex}, then {tex}\tan \theta{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}C\left(\alpha, \frac{7 \alpha-1}{4}\right){/tex} because {tex}C{/tex} lies on {tex}7 x-4 y-1=0{/tex}
Mid point of {tex}{AC}={M}\left(\frac{\alpha-3}{2}, \frac{7 \alpha+3}{8}\right){/tex}
Now, M lies on {tex}2 x+y-3=0{/tex},
{tex}2\left(\frac{\alpha-3}{2}\right)+\left(\frac{7 \alpha+3}{8}\right)-3=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=3{/tex} and {tex}\frac{7 \alpha-1}{4}=\frac{7(3)-1}{4}=5{/tex}
So, {tex}{C}=(3,5){/tex}
Now, {tex}{A}=(-3,1){/tex} and {tex}{C}=(3,5){/tex}
Slope of AC, {tex}\frac{y_{2}-y_{1}}{x_{2}-x_{1}}=\frac{5-1}{3+3}=\frac{4}{6}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
and slope of {tex}7 x-4 y-1=0{/tex} is {tex}\frac{7}{4}\left[\because y=\frac{7}{4} x-1\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \tan \frac{\theta}{2}=\left|\frac{m_{2}-m_{1}}{1+m_{2} m_{1}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{7}{4}-\frac{2}{3}}{1+\frac{7}{4}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
So, {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2}}{1-\tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{1-\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
